32	\section{Locally ringed spaces}
33	\label{section-locally-ringed-spaces}
34	
35	\noindent
36	Recall that we defined ringed spaces
37	in Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-ringed-spaces}.
38	Briefly, a ringed space is a pair $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ consisting
39	of a topological space $X$ and a sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$.
40	A morphism of ringed spaces $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
41	is given by a continuous map $f : X \to Y$ and an $f$-map of sheaves
42	of rings $f^\sharp : \mathcal{O}_Y \to \mathcal{O}_X$. You can
43	think of $f^\sharp$ as a map $\mathcal{O}_Y \to f_*\mathcal{O}_X$, see
44	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-f-map} and
45	Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-f-map}.
46	
47	\medskip\noindent
48	A good geometric example of this to keep in mind is
49	$\mathcal{C}^\infty$-manifolds and morphisms of
50	$\mathcal{C}^\infty$-manifolds. Namely, if $M$ is a
51	$\mathcal{C}^\infty$-manifold, then the sheaf $\mathcal{C}^\infty_M$
52	of smooth functions is a sheaf of rings on $M$. And any
53	map $f : M \to N$ of manifolds is smooth if and only if
54	for every local section $h$ of $\mathcal{C}^\infty_N$
55	the composition $h \circ f$ is a local section of $\mathcal{C}^\infty_M$.
56	Thus a smooth map $f$ gives rise in a natural way to a morphism
57	of ringed spaces
58	$$
59	f : (M , \mathcal{C}^\infty_M) \longrightarrow (N, \mathcal{C}^\infty_N)
60	$$
61	see Sheaves, Example \ref{sheaves-example-continuous-map-ringed}.
62	It is instructive to consider what happens to stalks. Namely, let
63	$m \in M$ with image $f(m) = n \in N$. Recall that the stalk
64	$\mathcal{C}^\infty_{M, m}$ is the ring of germs of smooth
65	functions at $m$, see
66	Sheaves, Example \ref{sheaves-example-germs-functions}.
67	The algebra of germs of functions on $(M, m)$ is a local ring with maximal
68	ideal the functions which vanish at $m$.
69	Similarly for $\mathcal{C}^\infty_{N, n}$. The map on stalks
70	$f^\sharp : \mathcal{C}^\infty_{N, n} \to \mathcal{C}^\infty_{M, m}$
71	maps the maximal ideal into the maximal ideal, simply
72	because $f(m) = n$.
73	
74	\medskip\noindent
75	In algebraic geometry we study schemes. On a scheme the sheaf of rings
76	is not determined by an intrinsic property of the space.
77	The spectrum of a ring $R$
78	(see Algebra, Section \ref{algebra-section-spectrum-ring}) endowed
79	with a sheaf of rings constructed out of $R$ (see below),
80	will be our basic building block. It will turn out that
81	the stalks of $\mathcal{O}$ on $\Spec(R)$ are the local
82	rings of $R$ at its primes. There are two reasons to introduce
83	locally ringed spaces in this setting: (1) There is in general
84	no mechanism that assigns to a continuous map of spectra a
85	map of the corresponding rings. This is why we add as an extra datum
86	the map $f^\sharp$. (2) If we consider morphisms
87	of these spectra in the category of ringed spaces, then the
88	maps on stalks may not be local homomorphisms. Since our
89	geometric intuition says it should we introduce locally ringed spaces
90	as follows.
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	\label{definition-locally-ringed-space}
94	Locally ringed spaces.
95	\begin{enumerate}
96	\item A {\it locally ringed space $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$}
97	is a pair consisting of a
98	topological space $X$ and a sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$ all of whose stalks
99	are local rings.
100	\item Given a locally ringed space $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ we say that
101	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is the {\it local ring of $X$ at $x$}.
102	We denote $\mathfrak{m}_{X, x}$ or simply $\mathfrak{m}_x$
103	the maximal ideal of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$. Moreover, the
104	{\it residue field of $X$ at $x$} is the residue field
105	$\kappa(x) = \mathcal{O}_{X, x}/\mathfrak{m}_x$.
106	\item A {\it morphism of locally ringed spaces}
107	$(f, f^\sharp) : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
108	is a morphism of ringed spaces such that for all $x \in X$
109	the induced ring map $\mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)} \to \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is a
110	local ring map.
111	\end{enumerate}
112	\end{definition}
113	
114	\noindent
115	We will usually suppress the sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$
116	in the notation when discussing locally ringed spaces. We will
117	simply refer to ``the locally ringed space $X$''.
118	We will by abuse of notation think of $X$ also as
119	the underlying topological space. Finally we will denote
120	the corresponding sheaf of rings
121	$\mathcal{O}_X$ as the {\it structure sheaf of $X$}.
122	In addition, it is customary to denote the maximal ideal
123	of the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ by
124	$\mathfrak{m}_{X, x}$ or simply $\mathfrak{m}_x$.
125	We will say ``let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of locally ringed
126	spaces'' thereby suppressing the structure sheaves even further.
127	In this case, we will by abuse of notation think of $f : X\to Y$
128	also as the underlying continuous map of topological spaces.
129	The $f$-map corresponding to $f$ will customarily
130	be denoted $f^\sharp$. The condition that $f$ is a morphism
131	of locally ringed spaces can then be expressed by saying that
132	for every $x\in X$ the map on stalks
133	$$
134	f^\sharp_x : \mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
135	$$
136	maps the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_{Y, f(x)}$ into
137	$\mathfrak m_{X, x}$.
138	
139	\medskip\noindent
140	Let us use these notational conventions to show that the
141	collection of locally ringed spaces and morphisms of locally
142	ringed spaces forms a category. In order to see this we have
143	to show that the composition of morphisms of locally ringed
144	spaces is a morphism of locally ringed spaces. OK, so let
145	$f : X \to Y$ and $g : Y \to Z$ be morphism of locally
146	ringed spaces. The composition of $f$ and $g$ is defined in
147	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-composition-maps-ringed-spaces}.
148	Let $x \in X$. By
149	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-compose-f-maps-stalks}
150	the composition
151	$$
152	\mathcal{O}_{Z, g(f(x))}
153	\xrightarrow{g^\sharp}
154	\mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)}
155	\xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
156	\mathcal{O}_{X, x}
157	$$
158	is the associated map on stalks for the morphism $g \circ f$.
159	The result follows since a composition of local ring
160	homomorphisms is a local ring homomorphism.
161	
162	\medskip\noindent
163	A pleasing feature of the definition is the fact that the functor
164	$$
165	\textit{Locally ringed spaces}
166	\longrightarrow
167	\textit{Ringed spaces}
168	$$
169	reflects isomorphisms (plus more).
170	Here is a less abstract statement.
171	
172	\begin{lemma}
173	\label{lemma-isomorphism-locally-ringed}
174	\begin{slogan}
175	An isomorphism of ringed spaces between locally ringed spaces is an
176	isomorphism of locally ringed spaces.
177	\end{slogan}
178	Let $X$, $Y$ be locally ringed spaces.
179	If $f : X \to Y$ is an isomorphism of
180	ringed spaces, then $f$ is an isomorphism
181	of locally ringed spaces.
182	\end{lemma}
183	
184	\begin{proof}
185	This follows trivially from the corresponding fact in algebra:
186	Suppose $A$, $B$ are local rings. Any isomorphism of rings
187	$A \to B$ is a local ring isomorphism.
188	\end{proof}

202	\section{Open immersions of locally ringed spaces}
203	\label{section-open-immersion}
204	
205	\begin{definition}
206	\label{definition-immersion-locally-ringed-spaces}
207	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
208	We say that $f$ is an {\it open immersion} if
209	$f$ is a homeomorphism of $X$ onto an open subset
210	of $Y$, and the map $f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_Y \to \mathcal{O}_X$
211	is an isomorphism.
212	\end{definition}
213	
214	\noindent
215	The following construction is parallel to
216	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-restriction} (3).
217	
218	\begin{example}
219	\label{example-open-subspace}
220	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
221	Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset.
222	Let $\mathcal{O}_U = \mathcal{O}_X|_U$
223	be the restriction of $\mathcal{O}_X$ to $U$.
224	For $u \in U$ the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{U, u}$
225	is equal to the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X, u}$, and hence is a
226	local ring. Thus $(U, \mathcal{O}_U)$ is a locally ringed
227	space and the morphism $j : (U, \mathcal{O}_U) \to (X, \mathcal{O}_X)$
228	is an open immersion.
229	\end{example}
230	
231	\begin{definition}
232	\label{definition-open-subspace}
233	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
234	Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset.
235	The locally ringed space $(U, \mathcal{O}_U)$
236	of Example \ref{example-open-subspace} above
237	is the {\it open subspace of $X$ associated to $U$}.
238	\end{definition}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	\label{lemma-open-immersion}
242	Let $f : X \to Y$ be an open immersion of
243	locally ringed spaces. Let $j : V = f(X) \to Y$
244	be the open subspace of $Y$ associated to the image of $f$.
245	There is a unique isomorphism $f' : X \cong V$ of
246	locally ringed spaces such that $f = j \circ f'$.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{proof}
250	Let $f'$ be the homeomorphism between $X$ and $V$ induced by $f$. Then
251	$f = j \circ f'$ as maps of topological spaces. Since there
252	is an isomorphism of sheaves
253	$f^\sharp : f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \mathcal{O}_X$, there is an isomorphism
254	of rings
255	$f^\sharp : \Gamma(U, f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_Y)) \to \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$
256	for each open subset $U \subset X$. Since
257	$\mathcal{O}_V = j^{-1}\mathcal{O}_Y$ and $f^{-1} = f'^{-1} j^{-1}$
258	(Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-pullback-composition}) we
259	see that $f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_Y = f'^{-1}\mathcal{O}_V$, hence
260	$\Gamma(U, f'^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_V)) \to \Gamma(U, f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_Y))$
261	is an isomorphism
262	for every $U \subset X$ open. By composing these we get an isomorphism of rings
263	$$
264	\Gamma(U, f'^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_V)) \to \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)
265	$$
266	for each open subset $U \subset X$, and therefore an isomorphism of sheaves
267	$f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_V) \to \mathcal{O}_X$. In other words, we have an
268	isomorphism $f'^{\sharp} : f'^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_V) \to \mathcal{O}_X$ and
269	therefore an isomorphism of locally ringed spaces
270	$(f', f'^{\sharp}) : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (V, \mathcal{O}_V)$
271	(use Lemma \ref{lemma-isomorphism-locally-ringed}).
272	Note that $f = j \circ f'$ as morphisms of locally ringed spaces
273	by construction.
274	
275	\medskip\noindent
276	Suppose we have another morphism
277	$f'' : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (V, \mathcal{O}_V)$ such that $f = j \circ f''$.
278	At any point $x \in X$, we have $j(f'(x)) = j(f''(x))$ from which it follows
279	that $f'(x) = f''(x)$ since $j$ is the inclusion map; therefore $f'$ and $f''$
280	are the same as morphisms of topological spaces. On structure sheaves,
281	for each open subset $U \subset X$ we have a commutative diagram
282	$$
283	\xymatrix @R=5em{
284	\Gamma(U, f^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_Y)) \ar[d]_\cong\ar[r]^\cong &
285	\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X) \\
286	\Gamma(U, f'^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_V)) \ar@/^/[ru]^{f'^\sharp}
287	\ar@/_/[ru]_{f''^\sharp} &
288	}
289	$$
290	from which we see that $f'^\sharp$ and $f''^\sharp$ define
291	the same morphism of sheaves.
292	\end{proof}
293	
294	\noindent
295	From now on we do not distinguish between open subsets
296	and their associated subspaces.
297	
298	\begin{lemma}
299	\label{lemma-restrict-map-to-opens}
300	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
301	Let $U \subset X$, and $V \subset Y$ be open subsets.
302	Suppose that $f(U) \subset V$. There exists a unique
303	morphism of locally ringed spaces $f|_U : U \to V$ such
304	that the following diagram is a commutative square of
305	locally ringed spaces
306	$$
307	\xymatrix{
308	U \ar[d]_{f|_U} \ar[r] & X \ar[d]^f \\
309	V \ar[r] & Y
310	}
311	$$
312	\end{lemma}
313	
314	\begin{proof}
315	Omitted.
316	\end{proof}
317	
318	\noindent
319	In the following we will use without further mention the following
320	fact which follows from the lemma above. Given any morphism
321	$f : Y \to X$ of locally ringed spaces, and any open subset
322	$U \subset X$ such that $f(Y) \subset U$, then there exists
323	a unique morphism of locally ringed spaces $Y \to U$ such that
324	the composition $Y \to U \to X$ is equal to $f$. In fact, we will
325	even by abuse of notation write $f : Y \to U$ since this rarely
326	gives rise to confusion.
327	
328	
329	
330	
331	
332	
333	
334	
335	
336	\section{Closed immersions of locally ringed spaces}
337	\label{section-closed-immersion}
338	
339	\noindent
340	We follow our conventions introduced in
341	Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-closed-immersion}.
342	
343	\begin{definition}
344	\label{definition-closed-immersion-locally-ringed-spaces}
345	Let $i : Z \to X$ be a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
346	We say that $i$ is a {\it closed immersion} if:
347	\begin{enumerate}
348	\item The map $i$ is a homeomorphism of $Z$ onto a closed subset of $X$.
349	\item The map $\mathcal{O}_X \to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z$ is surjective;
350	let $\mathcal{I}$ denote the kernel.
351	\item The $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{I}$
352	is locally generated by sections.
353	\end{enumerate}
354	\end{definition}
355	
356	\begin{lemma}
357	\label{lemma-closed-local-target}
358	Let $f : Z \to X$ be a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
359	In order for $f$ to be a closed immersion it suffices
360	that there exists an open covering $X = \bigcup U_i$ such
361	that each $f : f^{-1}U_i \to U_i$ is a closed immersion.
362	\end{lemma}
363	
364	\begin{proof}
365	Omitted.
366	\end{proof}
367	
368	\begin{example}
369	\label{example-closed-subspace}
370	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
371	Let $\mathcal{I} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$ be a sheaf
372	of ideals which is locally generated by sections as a sheaf
373	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules. Let $Z$ be the support of
374	the sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X/\mathcal{I}$.
375	This is a closed subset of $X$, by
376	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-support-sheaf-rings-closed}.
377	Denote $i : Z \to X$ the inclusion map.
378	By Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-i-star-exact}
379	there is a unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_Z$ on $Z$
380	with $i_*\mathcal{O}_Z = \mathcal{O}_X/\mathcal{I}$.
381	For any $z \in Z$ the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{Z, z}$
382	is equal to a quotient $\mathcal{O}_{X, i(z)}/\mathcal{I}_{i(z)}$
383	of a local ring and nonzero, hence a local ring.
384	Thus $i : (Z, \mathcal{O}_Z) \to (X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ is
385	a closed immersion of locally ringed spaces.
386	\end{example}
387	
388	\begin{definition}
389	\label{definition-closed-subspace}
390	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
391	Let $\mathcal{I}$ be a sheaf of ideals on $X$
392	which is locally generated by sections.
393	The locally ringed space $(Z, \mathcal{O}_Z)$
394	of Example \ref{example-closed-subspace} above
395	is the {\it closed subspace of $X$ associated to
396	the sheaf of ideals $\mathcal{I}$}.
397	\end{definition}
398	
399	\begin{lemma}
400	\label{lemma-closed-immersion}
401	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a closed immersion of
402	locally ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{I}$ be the
403	kernel of the map $\mathcal{O}_Y \to f_*\mathcal{O}_X$.
404	Let $i : Z \to Y$ be the closed subspace of $Y$
405	associated to $\mathcal{I}$.
406	There is a unique isomorphism $f' : X \cong Z$ of
407	locally ringed spaces such that $f = i \circ f'$.
408	\end{lemma}
409	
410	\begin{proof}
411	Omitted.
412	\end{proof}
413	
414	\begin{lemma}
415	\label{lemma-characterize-closed-subspace}
416	Let $X$, $Y$ be locally ringed spaces. Let
417	$\mathcal{I} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$ be a sheaf of ideals locally generated
418	by sections. Let $i : Z \to X$ be the associated closed subspace.
419	A morphism $f : Y \to X$ factors through $Z$ if and only if the map
420	$f^*\mathcal{I} \to f^*\mathcal{O}_X = \mathcal{O}_Y$
421	is zero. If this is the case the morphism $g : Y \to Z$
422	such that $f = i \circ g$ is unique.
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	Clearly if $f$ factors as $Y \to Z \to X$ then the map
427	$f^*\mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{O}_Y$ is zero. Conversely
428	suppose that $f^*\mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{O}_Y$ is zero.
429	Pick any $y \in Y$, and consider the ring map
430	$f^\sharp_y : \mathcal{O}_{X, f(y)} \to \mathcal{O}_{Y, y}$.
431	Since the composition
432	$\mathcal{I}_{f(y)} \to \mathcal{O}_{X, f(y)} \to \mathcal{O}_{Y, y}$
433	is zero by assumption and since $f^\sharp_y(1) = 1$
434	we see that $1 \not \in \mathcal{I}_{f(y)}$, i.e.,
435	$\mathcal{I}_{f(y)} \not = \mathcal{O}_{X, f(y)}$. We conclude that
436	$f(Y) \subset Z = \text{Supp}(\mathcal{O}_X/\mathcal{I})$.
437	Hence $f = i \circ g$ where $g : Y \to Z$ is continuous.
438	Consider the map $f^\sharp : \mathcal{O}_X \to f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$.
439	The assumption $f^*\mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{O}_Y$ is zero implies that
440	the composition $\mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{O}_X \to f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$ is
441	zero by adjointness of $f_*$ and $f^*$.
442	In other words, we obtain a morphism of sheaves of rings
443	$\overline{f^\sharp} : \mathcal{O}_X/\mathcal{I} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_Y$.
444	Note that $f_*\mathcal{O}_Y = i_*g_*\mathcal{O}_Y$ and
445	that $\mathcal{O}_X/\mathcal{I} = i_*\mathcal{O}_Z$.
446	By Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-equivalence-categories-closed-structures}
447	we obtain a unique morphism of sheaves of rings
448	$g^\sharp : \mathcal{O}_Z \to g_*\mathcal{O}_Y$ whose pushforward
449	under $i$ is $\overline{f^\sharp}$. We omit the verification that
450	$(g, g^\sharp)$ defines a morphism of locally ringed spaces
451	and that $f = i \circ g$ as a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
452	The uniqueness of $(g, g^\sharp)$ was pointed out above.
453	\end{proof}
454	
455	\begin{lemma}
456	\label{lemma-restrict-map-to-closed}
457	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of locally ringed spaces.
458	Let $\mathcal{I} \subset \mathcal{O}_Y$ be a sheaf of
459	ideals which is locally generated by sections.
460	Let $i : Z \to Y$ be the closed subspace associated to the
461	sheaf of ideals $\mathcal{I}$.
462	Let $\mathcal{J}$ be the image of the map
463	$f^*\mathcal{I} \to f^*\mathcal{O}_Y = \mathcal{O}_X$.
464	Then this ideal is locally generated by sections.
465	Moreover, let $i' : Z' \to X$ be the associated closed
466	subspace of $X$. There exists a unique
467	morphism of locally ringed spaces $f' : Z' \to Z$ such
468	that the following diagram is a commutative square of
469	locally ringed spaces
470	$$
471	\xymatrix{
472	Z' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{i'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
473	Z \ar[r]^{i} & Y
474	}
475	$$
476	Moreover, this diagram is a fibre square in the category of
477	locally ringed spaces.
478	\end{lemma}
479	
480	\begin{proof}
481	The ideal $\mathcal{J}$ is locally generated by sections
482	by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-locally-generated}.
483	The rest of the lemma follows from the characterization,
484	in Lemma \ref{lemma-characterize-closed-subspace} above,
485	of what it means for a morphism to factor through a closed
486	subspace.
487	\end{proof}

502	\section{Affine schemes}
503	\label{section-affine-schemes}
504	
505	\noindent
506	Let $R$ be a ring. Consider the topological space $\Spec(R)$
507	associated to $R$, see Algebra, Section \ref{algebra-section-spectrum-ring}.
508	We will endow this space with a sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
509	and the resulting pair $(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
510	will be an affine scheme.
511	
512	\medskip\noindent
513	Recall that $\Spec(R)$ has a basis of open sets $D(f)$,
514	$f \in R$ which we call standard opens, see Algebra,
515	Definition \ref{algebra-definition-Zariski-topology}.
516	In addition, the intersection of two standard opens is another:
517	$D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)$, $f, g\in R$.
518	
519	\begin{lemma}
520	\label{lemma-standard-open}
521	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $f \in R$.
522	\begin{enumerate}
523	\item If $g\in R$ and $D(g) \subset D(f)$, then
524	\begin{enumerate}
525	\item $f$ is invertible in $R_g$,
526	\item $g^e = af$ for some $e \geq 1$ and $a \in R$,
527	\item there is a canonical ring map $R_f \to R_g$, and
528	\item there is a canonical $R_f$-module map
529	$M_f \to M_g$ for any $R$-module $M$.
530	\end{enumerate}
531	\item Any open covering of $D(f)$ can be refined to a finite
532	open covering of the form $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$.
533	\item If $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$, then $D(f) \subset \bigcup D(g_i)$
534	if and only if $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal in $R_f$.
535	\end{enumerate}
536	\end{lemma}
537	
538	\begin{proof}
539	Recall that $D(g) = \Spec(R_g)$ (see
540	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-standard-open}).
541	Thus (a) holds because $f$
542	maps to an element of $R_g$ which is not
543	contained in any prime ideal, and hence invertible,
544	see Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-Zariski-topology}.
545	Write the inverse of $f$ in $R_g$ as $a/g^d$.
546	This means $g^d - af$ is annihilated by a power of $g$, whence (b).
547	For (c), the map $R_f \to R_g$ exists by (a) from the universal property
548	of localization, or we can define it by mapping $b/f^n$
549	to $a^nb/g^{ne}$. The equality $M_f = M \otimes_R R_f$
550	can be used to obtain the map on modules, or
551	we can define $M_f \to M_g$ by mapping
552	$x/f^n$ to $a^nx/g^{ne}$.
553	
554	\medskip\noindent
555	Recall that $D(f)$ is quasi-compact, see
556	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-qc-open}.
557	Hence the second statement follows directly
558	from the fact that the standard opens form
559	a basis for the topology.
560	
561	\medskip\noindent
562	The third statement follows directly from
563	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-Zariski-topology}.
564	\end{proof}
565	
566	\noindent
567	In Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-bases} we defined
568	the notion of a sheaf on a basis, and we showed that it is
569	essentially equivalent to the notion of a sheaf on the space,
570	see Sheaves, Lemmas \ref{sheaves-lemma-extend-off-basis} and
571	\ref{sheaves-lemma-extend-off-basis-structures}. Moreover,
572	we showed in
573	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-cofinal-systems-coverings-standard-case}
574	that it is sufficient to check the sheaf
575	condition on a cofinal system of open coverings for each
576	standard open. By the lemma above it suffices to check
577	on the finite coverings by standard opens.
578	
579	\begin{definition}
580	\label{definition-standard-covering}
581	Let $R$ be a ring.
582	\begin{enumerate}
583	\item A {\it standard open covering} of $\Spec(R)$
584	is a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$,
585	where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in R$.
586	\item Suppose that $D(f) \subset \Spec(R)$ is a standard
587	open. A {\it standard open covering} of $D(f)$
588	is a covering $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$,
589	where $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$.
590	\end{enumerate}
591	\end{definition}
592	
593	\noindent
594	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. We will define
595	a presheaf $\widetilde M$ on the basis of standard opens.
596	Suppose that $U \subset \Spec(R)$ is a standard open.
597	If $f, g \in R$ are such that $D(f) = D(g)$, then
598	by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above there are canonical
599	maps $M_f \to M_g$ and $M_g \to M_f$ which are mutually inverse.
600	Hence we may choose any $f$ such that $U = D(f)$
601	and define
602	$$
603	\widetilde M(U) = M_f.
604	$$
605	Note that if $D(g) \subset D(f)$, then by
606	Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above we have
607	a canonical map
608	$$
609	\widetilde M(D(f)) = M_f \longrightarrow M_g = \widetilde M(D(g)).
610	$$
611	Clearly, this defines a presheaf of abelian groups on the basis
612	of standard opens. If $M = R$, then $\widetilde R$ is a presheaf
613	of rings on the basis of standard opens.
614	
615	\medskip\noindent
616	Let us compute the stalk of $\widetilde M$ at a point $x \in \Spec(R)$.
617	Suppose that $x$ corresponds to the prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$.
618	By definition of the stalk we see that
619	$$
620	\widetilde M_x = \colim_{f\in R, f\not\in \mathfrak p} M_f
621	$$
622	Here the set $\{f \in R, f \not \in \mathfrak p\}$ is preordered by
623	the rule $f \geq f' \Leftrightarrow D(f) \subset D(f')$.
624	If $f_1, f_2 \in R \setminus \mathfrak p$, then we have
625	$f_1f_2 \geq f_1$ in this ordering. Hence by
626	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-localization-colimit}
627	we conclude that
628	$$
629	\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}.
630	$$
631	
632	\medskip\noindent
633	Next, we check the sheaf condition for the standard open coverings.
634	If $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$, then the sheaf condition
635	for this covering is equivalent with the exactness of the
636	sequence
637	$$
638	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{g_i} \to \bigoplus M_{g_ig_j}.
639	$$
640	Note that $D(g_i) = D(fg_i)$, and hence we can rewrite this
641	sequence as the sequence
642	$$
643	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{fg_i} \to \bigoplus M_{fg_ig_j}.
644	$$
645	In addition, by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above
646	we see that $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal
647	in $R_f$. Thus we may apply
648	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}
649	to the module $M_f$ over $R_f$ and the elements $g_1, \ldots, g_n$.
650	We conclude that the sequence is exact. By the remarks
651	made above, we see that $\widetilde M$ is a sheaf
652	on the basis of standard opens.
653	
654	\medskip\noindent
655	Thus we conclude from the material in
656	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-bases}
657	that there exists a
658	unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
659	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the standard opens.
660	Note that by our computation of stalks above, the
661	stalks of this sheaf of rings are all local rings.
662	
663	\medskip\noindent
664	Similarly, for any $R$-module $M$ there exists
665	a unique sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
666	$\mathcal{F}$ which agrees with $\widetilde M$ on the
667	standard opens, see
668	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-extend-off-basis-module}.
669	
670	\begin{definition}
671	\label{definition-structure-sheaf}
672	Let $R$ be a ring.
673	\begin{enumerate}
674	\item The {\it structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ of the
675	spectrum of $R$} is the unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
676	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the basis of standard opens.
677	\item The locally ringed space
678	$(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$ is called
679	the {\it spectrum} of $R$ and denoted $\Spec(R)$.
680	\item The sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
681	extending $\widetilde M$ to all opens of $\Spec(R)$
682	is called the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
683	associated to $M$. This sheaf is denoted $\widetilde M$ as
684	well.
685	\end{enumerate}
686	\end{definition}
687	
688	\noindent
689	We summarize the results obtained so far.
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	\label{lemma-spec-sheaves}
693	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
694	be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
695	associated to $M$.
696	\begin{enumerate}
697	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R$.
698	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \widetilde M) = M$ as an $R$-module.
699	\item For every $f \in R$ we have
700	$\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R_f$.
701	\item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
702	as an $R_f$-module.
703	\item Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
704	on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
705	are the maps
706	$R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
707	\ref{lemma-standard-open}.
708	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
709	be the corresponding point. We have
710	$\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R), x} = R_{\mathfrak p}$.
711	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
712	be the corresponding point. We have $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$
713	as an $R_{\mathfrak p}$-module.
714	\end{enumerate}
715	Moreover, all these identifications are functorial in the $R$
716	module $M$. In particular, the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
717	is an exact functor from the category of $R$-modules
718	to the category of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules.
719	\end{lemma}
720	
721	\begin{proof}
722	Assertions (1) - (7) are clear from the discussion above.
723	The exactness of the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
724	follows from the fact that the functor $M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak p}$
725	is exact and the fact that exactness of short exact sequences
726	may be checked on stalks, see
727	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-abelian}.
728	\end{proof}
729	
730	\begin{definition}
731	\label{definition-affine-scheme}
732	An {\it affine scheme} is a locally ringed space isomorphic
733	as a locally ringed space to $\Spec(R)$ for some ring $R$.
734	A {\it morphism of affine schemes} is a morphism in the category
735	of locally ringed spaces.
736	\end{definition}
737	
738	\noindent
739	It turns out that affine schemes play a special role
740	among all locally ringed spaces, which is what the next
741	section is about.
742	
743	
744	
745	
746	
747	
748	
749

759	
760	\section{The category of affine schemes}
761	\label{section-category-affine-schemes}
762	
763	\noindent
764	Note that if $Y$ is an affine scheme, then its
765	points are in canonical $1-1$ bijection with
766	prime ideals in $\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$.
767	
768	\begin{lemma}
769	\label{lemma-morphism-into-affine-where-point-goes}
770	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
771	Let $Y$ be an affine scheme.
772	Let $f \in \Mor(X, Y)$ be a morphism
773	of locally ringed spaces. Given a point $x \in X$
774	consider the ring maps
775	$$
776	\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
777	\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
778	$$
779	Let $\mathfrak p \subset \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ denote
780	the inverse image of $\mathfrak m_x$. Let $y \in Y$ be the
781	corresponding point. Then $f(x) = y$.
782	\end{lemma}
783	
784	\begin{proof}
785	Consider the commutative diagram
786	$$
787	\xymatrix{
788	\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \ar[r] &
789	\mathcal{O}_{X, x} \\
790	\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \ar[r] \ar[u] &
791	\mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)} \ar[u]
792	}
793	$$
794	(see the discussion of $f$-maps below
795	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-f-map}).
796	Since the right vertical arrow is local
797	we see that $\mathfrak m_{f(x)}$ is the
798	inverse image of $\mathfrak m_x$. The result
799	follows.
800	\end{proof}
801	
802	\begin{lemma}
803	\label{lemma-f-open}
804	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
805	Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
806	The set
807	$$
808	D(f) = \{x \in X \mid \text{image }f \not\in \mathfrak m_x\}
809	$$
810	is open. Moreover $f|_{D(f)}$ has an inverse.
811	\end{lemma}
812	
813	\begin{proof}
814	This is a special case of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-s-open}, but
815	we also give a direct proof.
816	Suppose that $U \subset X$ and $V \subset X$ are
817	two open subsets such that $f|_U$ has an inverse
818	$g$ and $f|_V$ has an inverse $h$. Then clearly
819	$g|_{U\cap V} = h|_{U\cap V}$. Thus it suffices
820	to show that $f$ is invertible in an open neighbourhood
821	of any $x \in D(f)$. This is clear because
822	$f \not \in \mathfrak m_x$ implies that $f \in \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
823	has an inverse $g \in \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ which means there
824	is some open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ so
825	that $g \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ and $g\cdot f|_U = 1$.
826	\end{proof}
827	
828	\begin{lemma}
829	\label{lemma-f-open-affine}
830	In Lemma \ref{lemma-f-open} above, if $X$ is an affine scheme,
831	then the open $D(f)$ agrees with the standard open $D(f)$
832	defined previously (in
833	Algebra, Definition \ref{algebra-definition-spectrum-ring}).
834	\end{lemma}
835	
836	\begin{proof}
837	Omitted.
838	\end{proof}
839	
840	\begin{lemma}
841	\label{lemma-morphism-into-affine}
842	\begin{reference}
843	A reference for this fact is \cite[II, Err 1, Prop. 1.8.1]{EGA}
844	where it is attributed to J. Tate.
845	\end{reference}
846	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
847	Let $Y$ be an affine scheme.
848	The map
849	$$
850	\Mor(X, Y)
851	\longrightarrow
852	\Hom(\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y), \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))
853	$$
854	which maps $f$ to $f^\sharp$ (on global sections) is bijective.
855	\end{lemma}
856	
857	\begin{proof}
858	Since $Y$ is affine we have
859	$(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \cong (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
860	for some ring $R$.
861	During the proof we will use facts about $Y$ and
862	its structure sheaf which are direct consequences of things
863	we know about the spectrum of a ring, see e.g.\ Lemma
864	\ref{lemma-spec-sheaves}.
865	
866	\medskip\noindent
867	Motivated by the lemmas above we construct the inverse map.
868	Let $\psi_Y : \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$
869	be a ring map. First, we define the corresponding map of
870	spaces
871	$$
872	\Psi : X \longrightarrow Y
873	$$
874	by the rule of
875	Lemma \ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine-where-point-goes}.
876	In other words, given $x \in X$ we define $\Psi(x)$
877	to be the point of $Y$ corresponding to the prime
878	in $\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ which is the inverse
879	image of $\mathfrak m_x$ under the composition
880	$
881	\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \xrightarrow{\psi_Y}
882	\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to
883	\mathcal{O}_{X, x}
884	$.
885	
886	\medskip\noindent
887	We claim that the map $\Psi : X \to Y$ is continuous.
888	The standard opens $D(g)$, for $g \in \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
889	are a basis for the topology of $Y$. Thus it suffices to prove
890	that $\Psi^{-1}(D(g))$ is open. By construction of $\Psi$
891	the inverse image $\Psi^{-1}(D(g))$ is exactly the set
892	$D(\psi_Y(g)) \subset X$ which is open by Lemma \ref{lemma-f-open}.
893	Hence $\Psi$ is continuous.
894	
895	\medskip\noindent
896	Next we construct a $\Psi$-map of sheaves from
897	$\mathcal{O}_Y$ to $\mathcal{O}_X$. By
898	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-f-map-basis-below-structures}
899	it suffices to define ring maps
900	$\psi_{D(g)} : \Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to
901	\Gamma(\Psi^{-1}(D(g)), \mathcal{O}_X)$
902	compatible with restriction maps.
903	We have a canonical isomorphism
904	$\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{O}_Y) = \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)_g$,
905	because $Y$ is an affine scheme.
906	Because $\psi_Y(g)$ is invertible on $D(\psi_Y(g))$
907	we see that there is a canonical map
908	$$
909	\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)_g
910	\longrightarrow
911	\Gamma(\Psi^{-1}(D(g)), \mathcal{O}_X)
912	=
913	\Gamma(D(\psi_Y(g)), \mathcal{O}_X)
914	$$
915	extending the map $\psi_Y$
916	by the universal property of localization.
917	Note that there is no choice but to take the canonical map here!
918	And we take this, combined
919	with the canonical identification
920	$\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{O}_Y) = \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)_g$, to
921	be $\psi_{D(g)}$. This is compatible with localization since the
922	restriction mapping on the affine schemes are defined in terms
923	of the universal properties of localization also, see
924	Lemmas \ref{lemma-spec-sheaves} and \ref{lemma-standard-open}.
925	
926	\medskip\noindent
927	Thus we have defined a morphism of ringed spaces
928	$(\Psi, \psi) : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
929	recovering $\psi_Y$ on global sections. To see that it is
930	a morphism of locally ringed spaces we have to show that
931	the induced maps on local rings
932	$$
933	\psi_x : \mathcal{O}_{Y, \Psi(x)} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
934	$$
935	are local. This follows immediately from the commutative diagram
936	of the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine-where-point-goes}
937	and the definition of $\Psi$.
938	
939	\medskip\noindent
940	Finally, we have to show that the constructions
941	$(\Psi, \psi) \mapsto \psi_Y$ and the construction
942	$\psi_Y \mapsto (\Psi, \psi)$ are inverse to each other.
943	Clearly, $\psi_Y \mapsto (\Psi, \psi) \mapsto \psi_Y$.
944	Hence the only thing to prove is that given $\psi_Y$
945	there is at most one pair $(\Psi, \psi)$ giving rise
946	to it. The uniqueness of $\Psi$ was shown in Lemma
947	\ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine-where-point-goes} and
948	given the uniqueness of $\Psi$ the uniqueness of the
949	map $\psi$ was pointed out during the course of the proof
950	above.
951	\end{proof}
952	
953	\begin{lemma}
954	\label{lemma-category-affine-schemes}
955	The category of affine schemes is equivalent to the opposite of the
956	category of rings. The equivalence is given by the functor that associates
957	to an affine scheme the global sections of its structure sheaf.
958	\end{lemma}
959	
960	\begin{proof}
961	This is now clear from Definition \ref{definition-affine-scheme}
962	and Lemma \ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
963	\end{proof}
964	
965	\begin{lemma}
966	\label{lemma-standard-open-affine}
967	Let $Y$ be an affine scheme.
968	Let $f \in \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$.
969	The open subspace $D(f)$ is an affine scheme.
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	We may assume that $Y = \Spec(R)$ and $f \in R$.
974	Consider the morphism of affine schemes
975	$\phi : U = \Spec(R_f) \to \Spec(R) = Y$ induced by the ring
976	map $R \to R_f$. By Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-standard-open}
977	we know that it is a homeomorphism onto $D(f)$.
978	On the other hand, the map $\phi^{-1}\mathcal{O}_Y \to \mathcal{O}_U$
979	is an isomorphism on stalks, hence an isomorphism. Thus we see
980	that $\phi$ is an open immersion. We conclude that $D(f)$
981	is isomorphic to $U$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-open-immersion}.
982	\end{proof}
983	
984	\begin{lemma}
985	\label{lemma-fibre-product-affine-schemes}
986	The category of affine schemes has finite products, and fibre products.
987	In other words, it has finite limits. Moreover, the products
988	and fibre products in the category of affine schemes
989	are the same as in the category of locally ringed spaces.
990	In a formula, we have (in the category of locally ringed spaces)
991	$$
992	\Spec(R) \times \Spec(S) =
993	\Spec(R \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} S)
994	$$
995	and given ring maps $R \to A$, $R \to B$ we have
996	$$
997	\Spec(A) \times_{\Spec(R)} \Spec(B)
998	=
999	\Spec(A \otimes_R B).
1000	$$
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	
1003	\begin{proof}
1004	This is just an application of Lemma \ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
1005	First of all, by that lemma, the affine scheme
1006	$\Spec(\mathbf{Z})$ is the final object in the category
1007	of locally ringed spaces. Thus the first displayed formula
1008	follows from the second. To prove the second note that
1009	for any locally ringed space $X$ we have
1010	\begin{eqnarray*}
1011	\Mor(X, \Spec(A \otimes_R B))
1012	& = &
1013	\Hom(A \otimes_R B, \mathcal{O}_X(X)) \\
1014	& = &
1015	\Hom(A, \mathcal{O}_X(X))
1016	\times_{\Hom(R, \mathcal{O}_X(X))}
1017	\Hom(B, \mathcal{O}_X(X)) \\
1018	& = &
1019	\Mor(X, \Spec(A))
1020	\times_{\Mor(X, \Spec(R))}
1021	\Mor(X, \Spec(B))
1022	\end{eqnarray*}
1023	which proves the formula.
1024	See Categories, Section \ref{categories-section-fibre-products} for the
1025	relevant definitions.
1026	\end{proof}
1027	
1028	\begin{lemma}
1029	\label{lemma-disjoint-union-affines}
1030	Let $X$ be a locally ringed space.
1031	Assume $X = U \amalg V$ with $U$ and $V$ open and
1032	such that $U$, $V$ are affine schemes. Then $X$ is an affine scheme.
1033	\end{lemma}
1034	
1035	\begin{proof}
1036	Set $R = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
1037	Note that $R = \mathcal{O}_X(U) \times \mathcal{O}_X(V)$
1038	by the sheaf property. By Lemma \ref{lemma-morphism-into-affine}
1039	there is a canonical morphism of locally ringed spaces
1040	$X \to \Spec(R)$. By Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-spec-product}
1041	we see that as a topological space
1042	$\Spec(\mathcal{O}_X(U)) \amalg \Spec(\mathcal{O}_X(V)) =
1043	\Spec(R)$
1044	with the maps coming from the ring homomorphisms
1045	$R \to \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ and $R \to \mathcal{O}_X(V)$.
1046	This of course means that $\Spec(R)$ is the coproduct
1047	in the category of locally ringed spaces as well.
1048	By assumption the morphism $X \to \Spec(R)$ induces an isomorphism
1049	of $\Spec(\mathcal{O}_X(U))$ with $U$ and similarly
1050	for $V$. Hence $X \to \Spec(R)$ is an isomorphism.
1051	\end{proof}
1052

1067	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines}
1068	\label{section-quasi-coherent-affine}
1069	
1070	\noindent
1071	Recall that we have defined the abstract notion of a quasi-coherent
1072	sheaf in Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-quasi-coherent}.
1073	In this section we show that any quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine
1074	scheme $\Spec(R)$ corresponds to the sheaf $\widetilde M$ associated to
1075	an $R$-module $M$.
1076	
1077	\begin{lemma}
1078	\label{lemma-compare-constructions}
1079	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1080	be an affine scheme. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. There exists a canonical
1081	isomorphism between the sheaf $\widetilde M$ associated to the $R$-module
1082	$M$ (Definition \ref{definition-structure-sheaf}) and the sheaf
1083	$\mathcal{F}_M$ associated to the $R$-module $M$
1084	(Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-sheaf-associated}).
1085	This isomorphism is functorial in $M$. In particular,
1086	the sheaves $\widetilde M$ are quasi-coherent. Moreover, they
1087	are characterized by the following mapping property
1088	$$
1089	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde M, \mathcal{F})
1090	=
1091	\Hom_R(M, \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}))
1092	$$
1093	for any sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$.
1094	Here a map $\alpha : \widetilde M \to \mathcal{F}$ corresponds
1095	to its effect on global sections.
1096	\end{lemma}
1097	
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	By Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-construct-quasi-coherent-sheaves}
1100	we have a morphism $\mathcal{F}_M \to \widetilde M$ corresponding
1101	to the map $M \to \Gamma(X, \widetilde M) = M$. Let $x \in X$
1102	correspond to the prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$.
1103	The induced map on stalks are the maps
1104	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x} \otimes_R M \to M_{\mathfrak p}$
1105	which are isomorphisms because
1106	$R_{\mathfrak p} \otimes_R M = M_{\mathfrak p}$.
1107	Hence the map $\mathcal{F}_M \to \widetilde M$ is an isomorphism.
1108	The mapping property follows from the mapping property of
1109	the sheaves $\mathcal{F}_M$.
1110	\end{proof}
1111	
1112	\begin{lemma}
1113	\label{lemma-widetilde-constructions}
1114	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1115	be an affine scheme. There are canonical isomorphisms
1116	\begin{enumerate}
1117	\item
1118	$
1119	\widetilde{M \otimes_R N}
1120	\cong
1121	\widetilde M \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \widetilde N
1122	$,
1123	see Modules, Section \ref{modules-section-tensor-product}.
1124	\item
1125	$
1126	\widetilde{\text{T}^n(M)}
1127	\cong
1128	\text{T}^n(\widetilde M)
1129	$,
1130	$
1131	\widetilde{\text{Sym}^n(M)}
1132	\cong
1133	\text{Sym}^n(\widetilde M)
1134	$, and
1135	$
1136	\widetilde{\wedge^n(M)}
1137	\cong
1138	\wedge^n(\widetilde M)
1139	$,
1140	see
1141	Modules, Section \ref{modules-section-symmetric-exterior}.
1142	\item if $M$ is a finitely presented $R$-module, then
1143	$
1144	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde M, \widetilde N)
1145	\cong
1146	\widetilde{\Hom_R(M,  N)}
1147	$,
1148	see
1149	Modules, Section \ref{modules-section-internal-hom}.
1150	\end{enumerate}
1151	\end{lemma}
1152	
1153	\begin{proof}[First proof]
1154	Using Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} and
1155	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-construct-quasi-coherent-sheaves}
1156	we see that the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$ can be viewed
1157	as $\pi^*$ for a morphism $\pi$ of ringed spaces.
1158	And pulling back modules commutes with tensor constructions by
1159	Modules, Lemmas \ref{modules-lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
1160	and \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-tensor-algebra}.
1161	The morphism $\pi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (\{*\}, R)$ is
1162	flat for example because the stalks of $\mathcal{O}_X$ are
1163	localizations of $R$ (Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-sheaves})
1164	and hence flat over $R$. Thus pullback by $\pi$ commutes
1165	with internal hom if the first module is finitely presented by
1166	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-internal-hom}.
1167	\end{proof}
1168	
1169	\begin{proof}[Second proof]
1170	Proof of (1). By Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} to give a map
1171	$\widetilde{M \otimes_R N}$ into
1172	$\widetilde M \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \widetilde N$
1173	we have to give a map on global sections
1174	$M \otimes_R N \to
1175	\Gamma(X, \widetilde M \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \widetilde N)$
1176	which exists by definition of the tensor product of sheaves
1177	of modules. To see that this map is an isomorphism it
1178	suffices to check that it is an isomorphism on stalks.
1179	And this follows from the description of the stalks
1180	of $\widetilde{M}$ (either in Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-sheaves} or in
1181	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-construct-quasi-coherent-sheaves}),
1182	the fact that tensor product commutes with localization
1183	(Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-tensor-product-localization}) and
1184	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.
1185	
1186	\medskip\noindent
1187	Proof of (2). This is similar to the proof of (1), using
1188	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-tensor-algebra-localization} and
1189	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-stalk-tensor-algebra}.
1190	
1191	\medskip\noindent
1192	Proof of (3). Since the construction $M \mapsto \widetilde{M}$ is functorial
1193	there is an $R$-linear map
1194	$\Hom_R(M, N) \to \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{M}, \widetilde{N})$.
1195	The target of this map is the global sections of
1196	$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde M, \widetilde N)$.
1197	Hence by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we obtain a map
1198	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde{\Hom_R(M,  N)} \to
1199	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde M, \widetilde N)$.
1200	We check that this is an isomorphism by comparing stalks.
1201	If $M$ is finitely presented as an $R$-module
1202	then $\widetilde M$ has a global finite presentation as an
1203	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Hence we conclude using
1204	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-hom-from-finitely-presented} and
1205	Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-stalk-internal-hom}.
1206	\end{proof}
1207	
1208	\begin{proof}[Third proof of part (1)]
1209	For any $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
1210	we have the following isomorphisms functorial in $M$, $N$, and $\mathcal{F}$
1211	\begin{align*}
1212	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{M} \otimes _{\mathcal{O} _X} \widetilde{N},
1213	\mathcal{F})
1214	& =
1215	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{M},
1216	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O} _X} (\widetilde{N}, \mathcal{F})) \\
1217	& =
1218	\Hom_R(M, \Gamma(X,
1219	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{N}, \mathcal{F}))) \\
1220	& =
1221	\Hom_R(M, \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{N}, \mathcal{F})) \\
1222	& =
1223	\Hom_R(M, \Hom_R(N, \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}))) \\
1224	& =
1225	\Hom_R(M \otimes_R N, \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})) \\
1226	& =
1227	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\widetilde{M \otimes_R N}, \mathcal{F})
1228	\end{align*}
1229	The first equality is Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-internal-hom}.
1230	The second equality is the universal property of $\widetilde{M}$, see
1231	Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}. The third equality holds by
1232	definition of $\SheafHom$. The fourth equality is the universal property
1233	of $\widetilde{N}$. Then fifth equality is
1234	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-hom-from-tensor-product}.
1235	The final equality is the universal property of
1236	$\widetilde{M \otimes_R N}$.
1237	By the Yoneda lemma (Categories, Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda})
1238	we obtain (1).
1239	\end{proof}
1240	
1241	\begin{lemma}
1242	\label{lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1243	Let
1244	$(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
1245	$(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1246	be affine schemes.
1247	Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
1248	morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
1249	$\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
1250	\begin{enumerate}
1251	\item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
1252	functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
1253	\item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
1254	in the $S$-module $N$.
1255	\end{enumerate}
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	
1258	\begin{proof}
1259	The first assertion follows from the identification in
1260	Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
1261	and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}.
1262	The second assertion follows from the fact
1263	that $\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))$ and hence
1264	$$
1265	\psi_* \widetilde N(D(f)) = \widetilde N(D(\psi^\sharp(f))) =
1266	N_{\psi^\sharp(f)} = (N_R)_f = \widetilde{N_R}(D(f))
1267	$$
1268	as desired.
1269	\end{proof}
1270	
1271	\noindent
1272	Lemma \ref{lemma-widetilde-pullback} above says in particular
1273	that if you restrict
1274	the sheaf $\widetilde M$ to a standard affine open subspace
1275	$D(f)$, then you get $\widetilde{M_f}$. We will use this from
1276	now on without further mention.
1277	
1278	\begin{lemma}
1279	\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
1280	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1281	be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a
1282	quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
1283	$\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to
1284	the $R$-module $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$.
1285	\end{lemma}
1286	
1287	\begin{proof}
1288	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1289	Since every standard open $D(f)$ is quasi-compact we see that
1290	$X$ is locally quasi-compact, i.e., every point has a fundamental
1291	system of quasi-compact neighbourhoods, see Topology,
1292	Definition \ref{topology-definition-locally-quasi-compact}.
1293	Hence by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-quasi-coherent-module}
1294	for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
1295	there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
1296	$\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
1297	sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
1298	In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
1299	By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
1300	covering to  a standard open covering.
1301	Thus we get a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup D(f_i)$
1302	and $R_{f_i}$-modules $M_i$ and isomorphisms
1303	$\varphi_i : \mathcal{F}|_{D(f_i)} \to \mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
1304	for some $R_{f_i}$-module $M_i$. On the overlaps
1305	we get isomorphisms
1306	$$
1307	\xymatrix{
1308	\mathcal{F}_{M_i}|_{D(f_if_j)}
1309	\ar[rr]^{\varphi_i^{-1}|_{D(f_if_j)}}
1310	& &
1311	\mathcal{F}|_{D(f_if_j)}
1312	\ar[rr]^{\varphi_j|_{D(f_if_j)}}
1313	& &
1314	\mathcal{F}_{M_j}|_{D(f_if_j)}.
1315	}
1316	$$
1317	Let us denote these $\psi_{ij}$. It is clear that
1318	we have the cocycle condition
1319	$$
1320	\psi_{jk}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1321	\circ
1322	\psi_{ij}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1323	=
1324	\psi_{ik}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1325	$$
1326	on triple overlaps.
1327	
1328	\medskip\noindent
1329	Recall that each of the open subspaces $D(f_i)$, $D(f_if_j)$,
1330	$D(f_if_jf_k)$ is an affine scheme. Hence the sheaves $\mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
1331	are isomorphic to the sheaves $\widetilde M_i$ by Lemma
1332	\ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above. In particular we see that
1333	$\mathcal{F}_{M_i}(D(f_if_j)) = (M_i)_{f_j}$, etc.
1334	Also by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above we see
1335	that $\psi_{ij}$ corresponds to a unique $R_{f_if_j}$-module isomorphism
1336	$$
1337	\psi_{ij} : (M_i)_{f_j} \longrightarrow (M_j)_{f_i}
1338	$$
1339	namely, the effect of $\psi_{ij}$ on sections over $D(f_if_j)$.
1340	Moreover these then satisfy the cocycle condition that
1341	$$
1342	\xymatrix{
1343	(M_i)_{f_jf_k}
1344	\ar[rd]_{\psi_{ij}}
1345	\ar[rr]^{\psi_{ik}}
1346	& &
1347	(M_k)_{f_if_j} \\
1348	&
1349	(M_j)_{f_if_k} \ar[ru]_{\psi_{jk}}
1350	}
1351	$$
1352	commutes (for any triple $i, j, k$).
1353	
1354	\medskip\noindent
1355	Now Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-glue-modules}
1356	shows that there exist an $R$-module $M$ such that
1357	$M_i = M_{f_i}$ compatible with the morphisms $\psi_{ij}$.
1358	Consider $\mathcal{F}_M = \widetilde M$. At this point it is
1359	a formality to show that $\widetilde M$ is isomorphic to
1360	the quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we started out with.
1361	Namely, the sheaves $\mathcal{F}$ and $\widetilde M$ give
1362	rise to isomorphic sets of glueing data of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1363	with respect to the covering $X = \bigcup D(f_i)$, see
1364	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves}
1365	and in particular Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-mapping-property-glue}.
1366	Explicitly, in the current situation, this boils down to
1367	the following argument: Let us construct an $R$-module map
1368	$$
1369	M \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}).
1370	$$
1371	Namely, given $m \in M$ we get $m_i = m/1 \in M_{f_i} = M_i$
1372	by construction of $M$. By construction of $M_i$ this corresponds
1373	to a section $s_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$. (Namely, $\varphi^{-1}_i(m_i)$.)
1374	We claim that $s_i|_{D(f_if_j)} = s_j|_{D(f_if_j)}$. This is
1375	true because, by construction of $M$, we have $\psi_{ij}(m_i) = m_j$,
1376	and by the construction of the $\psi_{ij}$. By the sheaf condition of
1377	$\mathcal{F}$ this collection of sections gives rise to a unique
1378	section $s$ of $\mathcal{F}$ over $X$. We leave it to the reader
1379	to show that $m \mapsto s$ is a $R$-module map.
1380	By Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we obtain an associated
1381	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module map
1382	$$
1383	\widetilde M \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}.
1384	$$
1385	By construction this map reduces to the isomorphisms
1386	$\varphi_i^{-1}$ on each $D(f_i)$ and hence is an isomorphism.
1387	\end{proof}
1388	
1389	\begin{lemma}
1390	\label{lemma-equivalence-quasi-coherent}
1391	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1392	be an affine scheme.
1393	The functors $M \mapsto \widetilde M$ and
1394	$\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$ define quasi-inverse
1395	equivalences of categories
1396	$$
1397	\xymatrix{
1398	\QCoh(\mathcal{O}_X)
1399	\ar@<1ex>[r]
1400	&
1401	\text{Mod}_R
1402	\ar@<1ex>[l]
1403	}
1404	$$
1405	between the category of quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1406	and the category of $R$-modules.
1407	\end{lemma}
1408	
1409	\begin{proof}
1410	See Lemmas \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
1411	and \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} above.
1412	\end{proof}
1413	
1414	\noindent
1415	From now on we will not distinguish between quasi-coherent
1416	sheaves on affine schemes and sheaves of the form $\widetilde M$.

1418	\begin{lemma}
1419	\label{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent}
1420	Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1421	Kernels and cokernels of maps of quasi-coherent
1422	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules are quasi-coherent.
1423	\end{lemma}
1424	
1425	\begin{proof}
1426	This follows from the exactness of the functor $\widetilde{\ }$
1427	since by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we know that any map
1428	$\psi : \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{N}$ comes from
1429	an $R$-module map $\varphi : M \to N$. (So we have
1430	$\Ker(\psi) = \widetilde{\Ker(\varphi)}$ and
1431	$\Coker(\psi) = \widetilde{\Coker(\varphi)}$.)
1432	\end{proof}

1434	\begin{lemma}
1435	\label{lemma-colimit-quasi-coherent}
1436	Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1437	The direct sum of an arbitrary collection of quasi-coherent sheaves
1438	on $X$ is quasi-coherent. The same holds for colimits.
1439	\end{lemma}
1440	
1441	\begin{proof}
1442	Suppose $\mathcal{F}_i$, $i \in I$ is a collection of quasi-coherent
1443	sheaves on $X$. By Lemma \ref{lemma-equivalence-quasi-coherent}
1444	above we can write $\mathcal{F}_i = \widetilde{M_i}$ for some $R$-module
1445	$M_i$. Set $M = \bigoplus M_i$. Consider the sheaf $\widetilde{M}$.
1446	For each standard open $D(f)$ we have
1447	$$
1448	\widetilde{M}(D(f)) = M_f =
1449	\left(\bigoplus M_i\right)_f =
1450	\bigoplus M_{i, f}.
1451	$$
1452	Hence we see that the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1453	$\widetilde{M}$ is the direct sum of the sheaves $\mathcal{F}_i$.
1454	A similar argument works for general colimits.
1455	\end{proof}

1464	\mathcal{F}_2 \to

1466	0
1467	$$
1468	is a short exact sequence of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1469	If two out of three are quasi-coherent then so is the third.
1470	\end{lemma}

1301	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,

1303	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that

1320	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1321	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1322	
1323	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1324	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1325	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1326	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1327	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1328	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1329	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1330	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1331	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1332	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1333	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1334	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1335	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1336	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1337	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1338	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1339	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1340	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1341	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1342	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1343	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1384	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1385	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1386	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1387	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1388	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1389	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1390	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1391	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1392	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1393	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1394	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1395	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1396	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1397	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1398	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1399	  \[
1400	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1401	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1402	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1403	  \]
1404	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1405	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1406	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1407	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1408	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1409	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1410	  \[
1411	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1412	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1413	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1414	  \]
1415	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1416	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1417	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1418	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1419	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1420	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1421	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1422	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1423	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1424	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1425	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1426	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1427	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1428	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1429	
1430	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1431	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1432	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1433	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1434	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1435	\end{definition}

1580	  \]
1581	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1582	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1583	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1584	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1585	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1586	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1587	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1588	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1589	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1590	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1591	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1592	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1593	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1594	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1595	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1596	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1597	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1598	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1599	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1600	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1601	\end{proof}

1603	\begin{definition}
1604	
1605	[The cover structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}\)]
1606	  \label{def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1607	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverStructurePresheaf,
1608	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeAug,
1609	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeComplexAug,
1610	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeComplexAug_f_zero,
1611	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial_δ_comp_aug,
1612	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFree_d_comp_aug,
1613	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFree_d_comp_factorThruImage,
1614	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaAug,

1619	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1620	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1200--1204).
1621	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1622	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1623	  % Let $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$
1624	  % be the image presheaf of the map
1625	  % $\bigoplus j_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_i} \to \mathcal{O}_X$."
1626	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1627	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1628	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1629	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1630	  \[
1631	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1632	  \]
1633	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1634	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1635	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1636	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1637	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1638	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1639	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1640	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1641	  presheaf.
1642	\end{definition}

1644	\begin{lemma}
1645	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1646	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1648	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1649	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1650	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1651	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1652	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1653	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1655	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1656	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1657	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1658	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1659	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1660	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1661	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1662	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1663	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1664	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1665	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1666	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1667	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1668	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1669	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1670	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1671	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1672	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1673	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1674	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1675	\end{lemma}
1676	
1677	\begin{proof}
1678	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1679	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1680	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1681	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1682	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1683	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1684	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1685	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1686	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1687	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1688	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1689	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1690	  quasi-isomorphism.
1691	\end{proof}

1693	\begin{lemma}
1694	
1695	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1696	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1698	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1699	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le}
1700	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1701	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1702	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1703	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1704	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1705	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1706	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1707	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1708	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1709	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1710	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1711	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1712	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1713	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1714	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1715	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1716	  \[
1717	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1718	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1719	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1720	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1721	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1722	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1723	  \]
1724	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1725	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1726	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1727	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1728	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1729	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1730	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1731	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1732	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1733	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1734	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1735	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1736	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1737	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1738	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1739	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by
1740	  \(\operatorname{rfl}\)) composed with
1741	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\) at \(V\). The full degreewise
1742	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)
1743	  (evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the coproduct, giving
1744	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_\sigma
1745	  (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\)),
1746	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\) (a surviving summand with
1747	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\)), and
1748	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\) (a summand with
1749	  \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) is the zero object); collapsing the coproduct over the
1750	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1751	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1752	\end{lemma}
1753	
1754	\begin{proof}
1755	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1756	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1757	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1758	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1759	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1760	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1761	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1762	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1763	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1764	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1765	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1766	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1767	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1768	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1769	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1770	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1771	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1772	\end{proof}

1778	\begin{lemma}
1779	[Constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech contracting-homotopy engine]
1780	  \label{lem:free_cech_engine}
1781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combDifferential,
1782	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combDifferential_comp,
1783	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combDifferential_eq_of_cocycle,

1785	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy,
1786	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy_spec,
1787	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy_zero,
1788	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.combSign_flip,
1789	    AlgebraicGeometry.FreeCechEngine.cons_comp_succAbove_succ,
1790	    AlgebraicGeometry.isZero_sigma_of_forall_isZero}
1791	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1792	  % (proof, the differential and contracting map of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1793	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1245--1268).
1794	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1795	  % is given by
1796	  % $$
1797	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
1798	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1799	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1800	  % $$
1801	  % The sum is finite as the element $s$ has finite support.
1802	  % Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1803	  % $$
1804	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1805	  % $$
1806	  % by the rule
1807	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1808	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}
1809	  % 0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
1810	  % s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
1811	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1812	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1813	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1814	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1815	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1816	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1817	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1818	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1819	  \[
1820	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1821	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1822	  \]
1823	  which squares to zero, \(\operatorname{combDifferential} \circ
1824	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1825	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1826	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1827	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1828	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1829	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1830	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1831	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are
1832	  sign- and index-bookkeeping consumed by the contracting identity: the
1833	  succ-shift reindexing \(\operatorname{cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) and the
1834	  alternating-sign flip \(\operatorname{combSign\_flip}\), together with the
1835	  degenerate-case simplification \(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_zero}\). The generic
1836	  category-theory helper \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) (a
1837	  coproduct of zero objects is a zero object) is recorded here as well, used to collapse
1838	  the empty-index direct sums of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}. This engine is
1839	  self-contained combinatorics on \(\operatorname{Fin.cons}\),
1840	  \(\operatorname{Fin.succAbove}\), \(\operatorname{Fin.predAbove}\), and
1841	  \(\operatorname{Finset.sum\_involution}\); it mirrors the contracting homotopy of the
1842	  Stacks \texttt{lemma-homology-complex} proof.
1843	\end{lemma}

1845	\begin{lemma}
1846	[Degreewise identification of the evaluated free complex with the engine complex]
1847	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}
1848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEvalEngineIso}

1850	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1851	  % (proof, the description of $K^{ext}_p(W)$ and its differential) (read from
1852	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1236--1251).
1853	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1854	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$
1855	  % and
1856	  % $$
1857	  % K^{ext}_p(W) =
1858	  % \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W).
1859	  % $$
1860	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1861	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$.
1862	  % Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1863	  % is given by
1864	  % $$
1865	  % d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
1866	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1867	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1868	  % $$"
