1870	  (the evaluated complex and its differential).}
1871	  Fix an open \(V\) with \(I_1(V) = \{\, i : V \leq U_i \,\} \neq \varnothing\). There
1872	  is an isomorphism of chain complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-modules
1873	  \[
1874	    \bigl((\operatorname{eval} V).\operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\bigr)
1875	      \bigl(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex} \mathcal{U}\bigr)
1876	    \;\cong\;
1877	    C_\bullet,
1878	    \qquad
1879	    C_p = \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1880	  \]
1881	  where the coproduct is taken in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(\mathcal{O}_X(V))\) and the
1882	  differential of \(C_\bullet\) is the constant-coefficient {\v C}ech differential
1883	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) on the index set \(J = I_1(V)\)
1884	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}). The isomorphism is \emph{degreewise} and
1885	  \emph{intertwines the evaluated alternating-face differential with}
1886	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\). It is the chain/coproduct dual of the section-side
1887	  cochain identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}; isolating it
1888	  here gives the empty and nonempty cases (Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
1889	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}) a common, ready degreewise model.
1890	\end{lemma}

1892	\begin{proof}
1893	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
1894	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
1895	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
1896	  coproduct,
1897	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
1898	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
1899	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
1900	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
1901	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
1902	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
1903	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
1904	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
1905	  C_p\).
1906	
1907	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
1908	  applied to the alternating face sum
1909	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
1910	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
1911	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
1912	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
1913	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
1914	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
1915	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
1916	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
1917	  identifies the evaluated differential with
1918	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
1919	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
1920	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
1921	\end{proof}

1923	\begin{lemma}
1924	
1925	[Evaluated free complex over an open meeting no cover member]
1926	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
1927	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_quasiIso_of_isEmpty,
1928	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
1929	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverStructurePresheaf_eval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
1930	    AlgebraicGeometry.isZero_homology_of_isZero_X}
1931	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
1932	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1933	  % (proof, the empty case) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
1934	  % L1228--1230).
1935	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $I_1 = \emptyset$, then the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W) = 0$
1936	  % so there is nothing to prove."
1937	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1938	  (empty case).}
1939	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}, suppose
1940	  \(I_1(V) = \varnothing\), i.e.\ \(V\) is contained in no cover member \(U_i\). Then
1941	  the evaluated augmented complex is identically zero: every degree-\(p\) term
1942	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) is the empty
1943	  direct sum \(0\) (there is no map \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \varnothing\)) and the
1944	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). Consequently the
1945	  evaluation at \(V\) of \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\) is a morphism of zero
1946	  complexes, which is a quasi-isomorphism (both sides have vanishing homology). The Lean
1947	  bricks are \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (each evaluated source
1948	  term is a zero object, via the coproduct-of-zeros helper
1949	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) of
1950	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}),
1951	  \(\operatorname{coverStructurePresheaf\_eval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (the evaluated
1952	  augmentation target is zero), and the generic
1953	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_homology\_of\_isZero\_X}\) (a short complex with zero middle
1954	  term has vanishing homology), which together force every
1955	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) to be a map of zero objects.
1956	\end{lemma}
1957	
1958	\begin{proof}
1959	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1960	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} each evaluated term is
1961	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\); when
1962	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\) the index type \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) is empty, so the
1963	  term is the zero object, and likewise \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). A
1964	  morphism whose source and target complexes are both zero (so all their homology
1965	  objects are zero, \(\operatorname{IsZero}\)) is a quasi-isomorphism; in Lean this is
1966	  immediate from \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\) since each
1967	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) is a map of zero objects, hence an isomorphism.
1968	\end{proof}

1970	\begin{lemma}
1971	[Prepend homotopy on the evaluated free complex]
1972	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy}
1973	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEvalPrependHomotopy}
1974	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso, lem:free_cech_engine}
1975	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1976	  % (proof, definition of the contracting map $h$) (read from
1977	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1253--1271).
1978	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1979	  % $h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)$ by the rule
1980	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1981	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}
1982	  % 0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
1983	  % s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
1984	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1985	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1986	  (the contracting map \(h\)).}
1987	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}, suppose
1988	  \(I_1(V) \neq \varnothing\) and fix an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\). The
1989	  \emph{prepend homotopy} is the family of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-linear maps
1990	  \[
1991	    h_p : K(\mathcal{U})_p(V) \longrightarrow K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1}(V),
1992	    \qquad
1993	    h_p(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} =
1994	      \begin{cases}
1995	        s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}} & i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}, \\
1996	        0 & i_0 \neq i_{\mathrm{fix}},
1997	      \end{cases}
1998	  \]
1999	  defined on the \(I_1\)-indexed direct sum of
2000	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} as the \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of
2001	  the per-summand prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) maps. This is exactly the
2002	  combinatorial content of \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy}\) of
2003	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}, here with the single
2004	  coefficient module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), transported across the degreewise
2005	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}. The maps \(h_p\) (together with the
2006	  degree \((-1)\!\to\!0\) map onto \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\))
2007	  are the homotopy hom maps assembled into a
2008	  \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.Homotopy}\) in
2009	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}.
2010	\end{lemma}
2011	
2012	\begin{proof}
2013	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
2014	  Under the identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} each
2015	  evaluated term is a finite direct sum of copies of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) indexed by
2016	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) prescription
2017	  sends the basis component at \((i_1, \ldots, i_{p+1})\) to the component at
2018	  \((i_{\mathrm{fix}}, i_1, \ldots, i_{p+1})\) and is \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-linear; it is
2019	  packaged as the \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of these per-summand maps, exactly as
2020	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy}\) is defined. This is a definition,
2021	  so there is nothing to prove beyond well-definedness, which is the linearity of
2022	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\).
2023	\end{proof}

2025	\begin{lemma}
2026	[The prepend homotopy contracts the augmented evaluated complex]
2027	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy_spec}
2028	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEvalPrependHomotopy_spec}
2029	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2030	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2031	  % (proof, the $dh + hd = \mathrm{id}$ computation) (read from
2032	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1272--1285).
2033	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Then we compute
2034	  % $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p} +
2035	  % \sum_{j \geq 1}\sum_{i \in I_1}
2036	  % (-1)^j (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}
2037	  % + (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) d(s)_{i_1 \ldots i_p}$
2038	  % which is equal to $s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ as desired."
2039	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2040	  (the contracting identity).}
2041	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy}
2042	  (\(I_1(V) \neq \varnothing\), \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\) fixed), the prepend
2043	  homotopy \(h\) satisfies the contracting identity
2044	  \[
2045	    d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}
2046	  \]
2047	  on the augmented evaluated complex
2048	  \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(V) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(V) \to
2049	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) \to 0\) (including the degree-\(0\)/degree-\((-1)\)
2050	  augmentation term). Here \(d\) is the alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential of
2051	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}.
2052	\end{lemma}
2053	
2054	\begin{proof}
2055	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2056	  This is the same alternating-sum cancellation as
2057	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\)
2058	  (\(\operatorname{combDifferential}(\operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, t) +
2059	  \operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, (\operatorname{combDifferential} t) = t\)),
2060	  specialised to coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Expanding
2061	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then
2062	  removes \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), returning \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\); every remaining term
2063	  of \(dh\) cancels in pairs against the corresponding term of \(hd\) (the
2064	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) of the differential), because removing an index
2065	  \(j \geq 1\) after prepending \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) equals prepending
2066	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) after removing index \(j-1\), with opposite signs. The net
2067	  result is \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), i.e.\ \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\); the index
2068	  bookkeeping is the \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) /
2069	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combSign\_flip}\) content of
2070	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}.
2071	\end{proof}

2081	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1224--1245).
2082	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In fact, we claim that for every $W \subset X$ the complex
2083	  % $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$ is homotopy equivalent to the zero complex."
2084	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2085	  (homotopy equivalence to zero).}
2086	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}, suppose
2087	  \(I_1(V) \neq \varnothing\). Then the evaluation at \(V\) of
2088	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\) is a quasi-isomorphism: the augmented
2089	  evaluated complex is contractible, hence its homology is concentrated in degree
2090	  \(0\) where it equals \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
2091	\end{lemma}

2093	\begin{proof}
2094	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy_spec}
2095	  By Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy}
2096	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy_spec} the degreewise prepend maps
2097	  \(h_p\) satisfy \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\) on the augmented evaluated complex, so they
2098	  assemble into a \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.Homotopy}\) between the identity
2099	  and the zero endomorphism of that complex. A complex whose identity is homotopic to
2100	  zero is contractible; equivalently the augmented evaluated complex is a
2101	  \(\operatorname{HomotopyEquiv}\) of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) with
2102	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V)[0] = \mathcal{O}_X(V)[0]\). A homotopy equivalence is a
2103	  quasi-isomorphism (\(\operatorname{Homotopy.toQuasiIso}\), or
2104	  \(\operatorname{HomotopyEquiv.toQuasiIso}\) /
2105	  \(\operatorname{QuasiIso.ofHomotopyEquiv}\)), so the evaluated augmentation map is a
2106	  quasi-isomorphism.
2107	\end{proof}

2217	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
2218	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2219	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.injective_of_adjoint}
2220	  \mathlibok
2221	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2222	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Injective.Basic}).}
2223	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2224	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2225	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.
2226	\end{lemma}

2228	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion of sheaves of modules]

2231	  \mathlibok
2232	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib

2234	  For the identity ring map \(\alpha = \mathbf{1}\) over a scheme \(X\),
2235	  sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion
2236	  \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
2237	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:

2240	    \qquad

2314	\begin{lemma}
2315	[Hom into an injective preserves exactness and quasi-isomorphisms]
2316	  \label{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective,
2318	    AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_map_preadditiveYoneda_of_injective}
2319	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2320	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category and \(I\) an injective object. Then the
2321	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) =
2322	  \operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2323	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2324	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2325	  finite colimits (\(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda\_obj\_preservesFiniteColimits\_of\_injective}\))
2326	  and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2327	  quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2328	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes
2329	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\)). Both statements
2330	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2332	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2333	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.
2334	\end{lemma}
2335	
2336	\begin{proof}
2337	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2338	  For injective \(I\), the characterisation
2339	  \(\operatorname{Injective.injective\_iff\_preservesEpimorphisms\_preadditiveYoneda\_obj}\)
2340	  gives that \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I\) preserves
2341	  epimorphisms. Combined with the automatic preservation of finite limits, the
2342	  short-exact-sequence criterion
2343	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesFiniteColimits\_iff\_forall\_exact\_map\_and\_epi}\)
2344	  shows it preserves finite colimits, hence (being also left exact) it has
2345	  \(\operatorname{PreservesHomology}\) by
2346	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact}\). A homology-preserving functor
2347	  sends quasi-isomorphisms to quasi-isomorphisms degreewise
2348	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_map\_of\_preservesHomology}\)), which is the second
2349	  statement.
2350	\end{proof}

2374	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
2375	  \texttt{lemma-injective-trivial-cech}.}
2376	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2377	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be any open covering, and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be an
2378	  \emph{injective} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the {\v C}ech cohomology of
2379	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is concentrated in degree zero:
2380	  \[
2381	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
2382	    \begin{cases}
2383	      \mathcal{I}(U) & p = 0, \\
2384	      0 & p > 0.
2385	    \end{cases}
2386	  \]
2387	\end{lemma}
2388	
2389	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "An injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is also injective as an
2390	% object in
2391	% the category $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ (for example since
2392	% sheafification is an exact left adjoint to the inclusion functor,
2393	% using Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-adjoint-preserve-injectives}).
2394	% Hence we can apply Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}
2395	% (or its proof) to see the result."
2396	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of
2397	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2398	% In this case the functor $\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})$
2399	% is exact on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. By
2400	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
2401	% $$
2402	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
2403	% =
2404	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
2405	% $$
2406	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
2407	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
2408	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
2409	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
2410	% $i > 0$."
2411	\begin{proof}
2412	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_op_identification, lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso, lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2413	  The argument has two independent parts.
2414	
2415	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2416	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2417	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2418	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2419	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2420	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2421	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2422	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2423	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2424	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2425	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2426	
2427	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2428	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2429	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2430	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2431	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2432	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2433	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2434	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2435	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2436	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2437	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2438	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2439	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2440	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2441	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2442	
2443	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely the exactness is produced by the
2444	  opposite-transport bridge. The augmentation \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}
2445	  \mathcal{U}\) is a quasi-isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso});
2446	  taking its opposite via the \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.op}\) machinery and
2447	  applying \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{I}\) keeps it a
2448	  quasi-isomorphism, since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules
2449	  (Part 1) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2450	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\),
2451	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2452	  mapped-opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isos
2453	  \(\operatorname{homCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2454	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification}) and
2455	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2456	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2457	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2458	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the mapped image of the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target
2459	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\). The latter has vanishing homology in positive
2460	  degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex does too. Hence
2461	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2462	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2463	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2464	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2465	\end{proof}

2468	[Surjectivity on sections from cofinal {\v C}ech-$H^1$ vanishing]
2469	  \label{lem:ses_cech_h1}
2470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ses_cech_h1}
2471	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
2472	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}
2473	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1592--1628).
2474	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a ringed space.
2475	  % Let
2476	  % $$
2477	  % 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
2478	  % $$
2479	  % be a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2480	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset.
2481	  % If there exists a cofinal system of open coverings $\mathcal{U}$
2482	  % of $U$ such that $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$,
2483	  % then the map $\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)$ is
2484	  % surjective."
2485	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
2486	  \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}.}
2487	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and let
2488	  \[
2489	    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
2490	  \]
2491	  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Let \(U \subset X\) be
2492	  open. If there is a cofinal system of open coverings \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(U\) with
2493	  \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\), then the map on sections
2494	  \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is surjective. Equivalently, the short exact
2495	  sequence is exact on sections over such \(U\).
2496	\end{lemma}
2497	
2498	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Take an element $s \in \mathcal{H}(U)$. Choose an open
2499	% covering
2500	% $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ such that
2501	% (a) $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ and (b)
2502	% $s|_{U_i}$ is the image of a section $s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)$.
2503	% Since we can certainly find $\mathcal{U}$ such that (b) holds
2504	% it follows from the assumptions of the lemma that we can find
2505	% $\mathcal{U}$ such that (a) and (b) both hold.
2506	% Consider the sections
2507	% $$
2508	% s_{i_0i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
2509	% $$
2510	% Since $s_i$ lifts $s$ we see that $s_{i_0i_1} \in \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0i_1})$.
2511	% By the vanishing of $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$ we can
2512	% find sections $t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$ such that
2513	% $$
2514	% s_{i_0i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
2515	% $$
2516	% Then clearly the sections $s_i - t_i$ satisfy the sheaf condition
2517	% and glue to a section of $\mathcal{G}$ over $U$ which maps to $s$.
2518	% Hence we win."
2519	\begin{proof}
2520	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
2521	  Let \(s \in \mathcal{H}(U)\). Choose an open covering \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2522	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) fine enough that (a) \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},
2523	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
2524	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the
2525	  coverings with property (a) are cofinal and (b) holds on any sufficiently fine
2526	  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).
2527	  On the double overlaps form
2528	  \[
2529	    s_{i_0 i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}.
2530	  \]
2531	  Because the \(s_i\) all lift the single section \(s\), the difference
2532	  \(s_{i_0 i_1}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\mathcal{H}\), hence lies in the subsheaf
2533	  \(\mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 i_1})\); the family \((s_{i_0 i_1})\) is a {\v C}ech
2534	  \(1\)-cocycle for \(\mathcal{F}\). By \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
2535	  it is a coboundary: there are \(t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)\) with
2536	  \(s_{i_0 i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}\). Then the
2537	  corrected sections \(s_i - t_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) agree on overlaps, so by the
2538	  sheaf condition they glue to a global section of \(\mathcal{G}\) over \(U\) whose
2539	  image in \(\mathcal{H}\) is \(s\). Hence \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is
2540	  surjective.
2541	\end{proof}

2599	[{\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis]
2600	  \label{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}

2602	  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
2603	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma

2605	  % basis-comparison criterion for {\v C}ech vs.\ sheaf cohomology), statement and

2607	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(Variant of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-vanish}.)

2609	  % Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for the topology on $X$.

2612	  % with the following properties:
2613	  % \begin{enumerate}

2624	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
2625	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.}
2626	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, let
2627	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and suppose there is a set
2628	  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of open coverings such that: (1) every \(\mathcal{U} :
2629	  U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) has \(U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}\) and
2630	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\); (2) for each
2631	  \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(U\) occurring in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
2632	  cofinal among all open coverings of \(U\); and (3) for every \(\mathcal{U} \in
2633	  \mathrm{Cov}\) the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
2634	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). Then
2635	  \[
2636	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0 \text{ and any }
2637	      U \in \mathcal{B}.
2638	  \]
2639	  This is the basis-comparison vehicle that upgrades the standard-cover {\v C}ech
2640	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} to the Serre vanishing of
2641	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}: instantiating \(\mathcal{B}\) as the affine
2642	  opens and \(\mathrm{Cov}\) as the standard affine covers, condition (3) is exactly
2643	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. The formal target of this lemma is the
2644	  \emph{vanishing} conclusion \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\) under

2646	  \mathcal{F}) \cong H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of {\v C}ech and sheaf cohomology; only
2647	  the positive-degree vanishing is needed downstream, and it is all the statement
2648	  establishes.

2711	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)} if condition (3) holds for it. We argue by a
2712	  dimension shift, taking \(\mathcal{F}\) (with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
2713	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) as the inductive variable and using no input from
2714	  affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing.

2717	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\). By

2719	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every cover, in
2720	  particular for every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). Put \(\mathcal{Q} =
2721	  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving the short exact sequence
2722	  \[

2724	  \]
2725	  For each \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are cofinal among
2726	  coverings of \(U\) (condition (2)) and \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
2727	  0\) for them, so Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the sequence exact on sections,
2728	  \[
2729	    0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0,
2730	    \qquad U \in \mathcal{B}.
2731	  \]
2732	  Since by condition (1) every term of a {\v C}ech complex for a cover
2733	  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) is a product of values over elements of
2734	  \(\mathcal{B}\), this section-level exactness yields a short exact sequence of
2735	  {\v C}ech complexes
2736	  \[
2737	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
2738	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
2739	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0,
2740	  \]
2741	  and hence a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology. As
2742	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
2743	  0\) for \(p > 0\), the long exact sequence forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
2744	  \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\): the quotient \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has
2745	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).
2746

2748	  \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) over a fixed \(U \in \mathcal{B}\). The
2749	  injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has \(H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(n > 0\). The
2750	  section-level surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) = H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U,
2751	  \mathcal{Q}) = \mathcal{Q}(U)\) established above makes the connecting map into
2752	  \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F})\) zero, whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Applying the
2753	  same conclusion to \(\mathcal{Q}\) (another module with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
2754	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) gives \(H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\), and then the
2755	  long exact sequence with \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = H^2(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) forces
2756	  \(H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Iterating the dimension shift — at each stage
2757	  \(\mathcal{Q}\) plays the role of \(\mathcal{F}\) — gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) =
2758	  0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).
2759	
2760	  The argument never uses affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing
2761	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}); its only {\v C}ech-vanishing input is
2762	  condition (3), which on the affine instantiation is supplied by the standard-cover
2763	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. Thus the dependency
2764	  \(\text{affine Serre vanishing} \to \text{this lemma}\) is one-directional and the
2765	  graph is acyclic.
2766	\end{proof}

2768	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
2769	
2770	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
2771	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
2772	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
2773	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
2774	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
2775	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.
2776	
2777	\begin{lemma}
2778	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
2779	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
2780	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
2781	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
2782	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
2783	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
2784	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
2785	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
2786	  % \begin{equation}
2787	  % \label{equation-extended}
2788	  % 0 \to
2789	  % M \to
2790	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
2791	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
2792	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
2793	  % \ldots
2794	  % \end{equation}
2795	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
2796	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
2797	  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
2798	  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
2799	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
2800	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
2801	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
2802	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
2803	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
2804	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
2805	  \[
2806	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
2807	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
2808	    \qquad
2809	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
2810	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
2811	  \]
2812	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
2813	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
2814	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
2815	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
2816	\end{lemma}
2817	
2818	\begin{proof}
2819	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
2820	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
2821	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
2822	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
2823	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
2824	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
2825	  localisations
2826	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
2827	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
2828	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
2829	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
2830	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
2831	\end{proof}

2833	\begin{lemma}
2834	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
2835	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
2836	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
2837	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
2838	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
2839	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
2840	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
2841	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
2842	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
2843	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
2844	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
2845	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
2846	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
2847	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
2848	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
2849	  \[
2850	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
2851	      \;\cong\;
2852	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
2853	  \]
2854	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
2855	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
2856	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
2857	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
2858	\end{lemma}
2859	
2860	\begin{proof}
2861	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
2862	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
2863	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
2864	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
2865	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
2866	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
2867	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
2868	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
2869	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
2870	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
2871	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
2872	  additive homology-preserving functor.
2873	\end{proof}

2876	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]

2882	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
2883	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
2884	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
2885	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
2886	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
2887	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
2888	  % associated to the presheaves
2889	  % $$
2890	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
2891	  % $$
2892	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
2893	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
2894	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
2895	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
2896	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
2897	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
2898	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
2899	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
2900	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
2901	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
2902	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
2903	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
2904	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
2905	  \[
2906	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
2907	      \;\cong\;
2908	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
2909	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
2910	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
2911	  \]
2912	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
2913	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
2914	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream
2915	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} and
2916	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
2917	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
2918	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
2919	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
2920	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
2921	
2922	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
2923	  of the preimage,
2924	  \[
2925	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
2926	      \;=\;
2927	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
2928	  \]
2929	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
2930	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the
2931	  resolution-internal sheafify-of-objectwise-homology form.
2932	\end{lemma}

2934	\begin{proof}
2935	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
2936	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
2937	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
2938	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
2939	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
2940	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
2941	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
2942	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
2943	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
2944	  \[
2945	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
2946	      \;\cong\;
2947	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
2948	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
2949	  \]
2950	  which is the stated isomorphism. Since the sheafification of a presheaf vanishes
2951	  exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens, this yields the
2952	  affine-local vanishing criterion.
2953	\end{proof}

2955	\begin{lemma}
2956	[Affine open immersions are $(\cdot)_*$-acyclic and degenerate the composition]
2957	  \label{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
2958	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_openImmersion_comp}
2959	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
2960	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma

2964	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.

2968	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
2969	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
2970	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
2971	  \to S\). Then:
2972	  \begin{enumerate}
2973	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
2974	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
2975	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
2976	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
2977	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
2978	      \(k \geq 0\),
2979	      \[
2980	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
2981	      \]
2982	  \end{enumerate}
2983	\end{lemma}

2989	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.

2991	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.

2998	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
2999	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
3000	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
3001	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
3002	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
3003	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
3004	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
3005	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
3006	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
3007	
3008	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in

**Chapter:** `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`
**Scope:** Four new/edited blocks from the blueprint-writer round:
`lem:section_cech_objd_apply`, `lem:section_cech_coface_match`,
`lem:section_cech_ab_exact`, `lem:section_cech_product_equiv`.

---

## Changes made

### 1. `lem:section_cech_coface_match` — statement purity

**Old:** "... with an \(R\)-module \(M\) **(the active target; see the note below on a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\))**."

**New:** "... with an \(R\)-module \(M\) (for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\), see the note following the statement)."

Rationale: "active target" is project-progress language; replaced with a timeless parenthetical directing the reader to the note.

---

### 2. `% NOTE:` following `lem:section_cech_coface_match` — project-history & implementation-type removal

**Removed language:**
- "the active target is the tilde case" (project-progress)
- "the per-coordinate AddEquiv to LocalizedModule" (Lean implementation type `AddEquiv`)
- "separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in def:qcoh_sections_localized" (project-history)

**Replacement:** Restated as timeless mathematics:
> For the tilde case \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the per-coordinate comparison isomorphism and the naturality square follow directly from `qcohRestriction_eq_comparison`. For a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\), reduce to the tilde case via \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag 01I8), the affine equivalence \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), which is the open gap recorded in Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.

Mathematical content is fully preserved; only framing language changed.

---

### 3. `lem:section_cech_coface_match` proof — last paragraph replacement

**Removed (Lean-implementation paragraph):**
> "The infrastructure this step owns is the accessor reconciliation: the section {\v C}ech complex reads sections through the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued accessor `((toPresheafOfModules(Spec R)).obj F).presheaf.obj(op(D(s_σ)))`, whereas the proved localisation facts of Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} are stated for the \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\)-valued sections of `modulesSpecToSheaf` / `tilde.toOpen`. Reconciling these two views ... into the single \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) \(\varphi_\sigma\) ..."

Problems: "infrastructure this step owns", raw Lean term path `(toPresheafOfModules(...)).obj.presheaf.obj(op(...))`, `Ab`-valued vs `ModuleCat`-valued framing, `AddEquiv` — all implementation-level language.

**Replacement (timeless mathematics):**
> The isomorphism \(\varphi_\sigma\) is obtained by matching the two descriptions of the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(s_\sigma)\): the cosimplicial functor provides one description and the localisation property of Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} provides the other. Their agreement, together with the naturality square, is the content of this bridge.

---

### 4. `lem:section_cech_product_equiv` statement — proof-strategy rationale removal

**Removed tail:** "so that downstream proofs may rewrite by it without unfolding the \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) coercion."

Rationale: "downstream proofs may rewrite by it without unfolding" is proof-assistant strategy language, not mathematics. The definitional restatement of `sectionCechProductEquiv_apply` is described mathematically; the implementation motivation for its existence is not blueprint content.

---

## Unchanged blocks

- `lem:section_cech_objd_apply` — already clean; all names reference canonical mathematical operations.
- `lem:section_cech_ab_exact` — "discharges that hypothesis" and "It chains [lemma1], [lemma2]" are informal blueprint proof-sketch language, not tactic strings; left as-is.
- All FreePresheafComplex / P4 / P5 blocks — not touched.
- All `\leanok` markers — not touched.

---

## Tilde-bridge prose verification

`lem:section_cech_coface_match` proof step (ii) "Tilde \(\mathcal{F}\)-bridge" reads as timeless mathematics:

1. **Per-coordinate localisation isomorphism φ_σ** — uniquely determined by the two `IsLocalizedModule` structures (both localise \(M\) at \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)), established via `IsLocalizedModule.iso` and uniqueness `AwayComparison.comparison_unique`. ✅ No implementation TODO.

2. **Naturality square** — \(\varphi_\sigma \circ \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) = \operatorname{dCoface} \circ \varphi_{\sigma \circ d_i}\) is exactly `qcohRestriction_eq_comparison` from Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the presheaf restriction of \(\widetilde{M}\) equals the away-localisation comparison. ✅ Timeless.

---

## Citation discipline

No missing `% SOURCE QUOTE` entries found. The four lemmas are technical intermediates derived from:
- `def:qcoh_sections_localized` (already cites Stacks 01HV items (4)–(5))
- `lem:section_cech_homology_exact` (already cites Stacks `lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`)
